perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information excludes: (i) Information that at the date of disclosure to you or your Representatives is publicly known or at any time after that date becomes publicly known or otherwise becomes available to you (otherwise than as a consequence of any breach of this agreement by you (or would constitute a breach by your Representatives , if they were a party) or which you know (or ought reasonably to have known having made reasonable enquiry) to have been disclosed in breach of any duty of confidentiality owed to us or any member of our Group);
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information excludes: (i) Information that at the date of disclosure to you or your Representatives is publicly known or at any time after that date becomes publicly known or otherwise becomes available to you, regardless of the reason or source of the information becoming publicly known;
perturbation.explanation: The original text excludes information that becomes publicly known *except* through a breach of the agreement. The modified text removes this exception, thus including information disclosed through a breach as non-confidential. This could contradict laws protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines trade secrets and provides protection against misappropriation. By allowing information disclosed through a breach of confidentiality to be considered non-confidential, the modified text undermines the protection afforded to trade secrets under the DTSA.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original contract text excludes information that becomes publicly known through a breach of the agreement from being considered non-confidential. The modified text removes this exception. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the protection afforded to trade secrets under the DTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines terms related to trade secret protection, and while it doesn't explicitly state that information disclosed through a breach remains confidential, the fact that trade secrets are defined and protected implies that unauthorized disclosure is a violation, which the original contract acknowledged but the modified one does not, creating a contradiction by weakening trade secret protections.

perturbation.original_text: Representatives " means the directors, off icers, employees, agents and professional advisers of a party or any member of such party's Group from time to time and any Permitted Finance Providers
perturbation.changed_text: Representatives " means any individual acting on behalf of a party, regardless of employment status or formal agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition limits 'Representatives' to specific roles. The modified definition broadens it to include *any* individual acting on behalf of a party. This could create confusion regarding who is bound by the agreement, especially concerning vicarious liability under agency law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Agency law requires specific conditions (manifestation of consent, control by the principal) for an agency relationship. The modified definition of 'Representatives' disregards these requirements, potentially imposing obligations on individuals who are not legally agents.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Representatives' beyond legally recognized agency relationships. The scraped snippet defines agency as 'a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals' and states that 'A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal.' The original text limits representatives to specific roles (directors, officers, etc.), aligning more closely with potential agency relationships where authority can be clearly established. The changed text, however, includes 'any individual acting on behalf of a party, regardless of employment status or formal agreement,' which undermines the requirement for authority and control inherent in agency law. This could lead to vicarious liability issues where a party is held responsible for the actions of someone who is not a legally recognized agent, thus contradicting the established principles of agency law.

perturbation.original_text: You shall treat and keep all Confidential Information as confidential and shall not, without our prior written consent, dir ectly or indirectly disclose Confidential Information to any other person other than as permitted by paragraph 3.1. You shall ensure that the Confi dential Information is protected with the same security measures and degree of care that would apply to your own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: You shall endeavor to treat Confidential Information as confidential, but disclosure is permitted at your discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates confidentiality. The changed clause makes it discretionary, weakening the confidentiality obligation. This could conflict with trade secret laws requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The 'discretionary' disclosure clause contradicts the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, thus jeopardizing trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates confidentiality, while the changed text allows for discretionary disclosure. This undermines the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets don't directly define 'reasonable efforts', the Uniform Law Commission's purpose, as stated in Snippet 2, is to bring clarity and stability to state statutory law. The discretionary disclosure clause contradicts the intent of maintaining clarity and stability around trade secret protection, which requires a more stringent confidentiality obligation than what the changed text provides. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition because 'discretionary disclosure' deviates from legally recognized definitions of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall continue for 18 months after the date of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall continue until either party deems it no longer necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies a fixed duration. The changed clause makes the duration indefinite and dependent on the parties' subjective assessment. This introduces uncertainty and could be challenged if it unreasonably restricts someone's ability to engage in lawful activities, potentially conflicting with restraint of trade principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restraint of trade doctrines limit the enforceability of agreements that unduly restrict a person's ability to conduct business or exercise their profession. An indefinite NDA, terminable at will, could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if it prevents someone from using general knowledge or skills acquired during the period covered by the NDA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: restraint of trade | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restraint of trade A restraint of trade is any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The term is most commonly used in the context of government antitrust regulation. For example, federally, 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations.” For example, in American Needle, Inc. v. Nat’l Football League , the U.S. Supreme Court held that licensing activities for individual teams’ intellectual property conducted through a corporation separate from the teams with its own management [i.e. the NFL] fell within a possible restraint of trade covered under 15 U.S.C. § 1. Many states also have their own local restraints on anti-competitive business activity. For example, Massachusetts General Law Ch. 93 § 4 states “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce in the commonwealth shall be unlawful.” [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities unfair competition wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a fixed duration of 18 months, providing certainty. The changed text allows the agreement to continue until either party 'deems it no longer necessary,' creating an indefinite duration terminable at will. This could potentially restrain trade, as the Law Explanation suggests. The scraped law snippet 1 defines 'restraint of trade' as 'any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions.' The changed text, by creating an indefinite and subjective termination clause, could limit a party's ability to engage in future transactions, especially if the agreement involves proprietary information or client relationships, thus contradicting principles against restraint of trade.
